content.assessment: From a fiscal and incentive standpoint, status quo monitoring is nearly costless in direct fiscal terms since it changes no funding flows or admissions mechanisms, but this apparent affordability masks a hidden opportunity cost: continued misallocation of already-equal per-pupil resources (F-live-education_finance-07) toward a system that concentrates advantage (F-live-education_finance-04) without corrective incentive change. The scale is modest (F-live-education_finance-01), so the direct fiscal exposure is small, but the value-for-money case is weak because monitoring infrastructure exists without a policy-correction track record.
opportunities: Low-cost data infrastructure could be repurposed for future costed reforms without new investment
strengths: No new fiscal outlay required to maintain current admissions and funding structure
Existing PISA/National ABC reporting infrastructure provides a low-cost data feed on SES-linked outcomes
threats: Historical precedent that PISA/monitoring data has not translated into policy correction suggests continued fiscal drift toward entrenched misallocation
Demographic contraction and geographic concentration risk increasing the per-pupil cost of maintaining segregated elite capacity relative to shrinking comprehensive enrollment
weaknesses: Identical per-pupil financing across advantaged and disadvantaged schools already fails to correct for private-tutoring-driven access gaps, and monitoring alone does not change this incidence
Small system scale (22,000-25,000 students) means the equity distortion is concentrated but persistent, offering poor value for money relative to its social cost
